turers produce supplements marketed for joint support.
Although collagen is often considered to be more impor-
Gluteal tendon calcification tant in soft tissues, the musculoskeletal consequences of 
vitamin C deficiency can include enlarged costochondral 
This is an incidental finding on radiography and causes no 
junctions, abnormal and delayed calcification of epiphy-
clinical signs (see Figure 23.2).
seal growth plates, bony microfractures, bone loss and 
altered dentin. Haemarthrosis can occur and myopathy, 
demonstrated by ultrastructural muscle disorganization, 
has been documented.
Affected animals often present with a hunched stance 
and may have a shifting lameness or show signs of gen-
eralized pain. Dental disease can occur as a result of 
reduced mastication secondary to pain. Dental changes 
may not be reversible. There can be palpable swelling 
around the joints and a reduced range of joint motion. The 
clinical signs can relate to bony changes, muscle changes 
or joint effusion. Because the response to treatment is 
usually so rapid, a treatment trial is often the first step, in 
combination with analgesia. An audit of the patient’s cur-
rent vitamin C supplementation is advisable, with care 
taken to differentiate between what is offered to the animal 
and what it actually eats. Treatment usually starts with 
high-dose vitamin C supplementation, then reduces to 
normal supplementation levels once the clinical signs have 
resolved (see Appendix 2). There may be permanent loss 
of function in the case of significant bone pathology, so 
radiographs are recommended if the animal does not 
improve. Animals with muscle fibrosis may also fail to 
23.2 Radiograph showing osteopenia, periosteal thickening  respond to treatment. It is not uncommon to see radio-
consistent with fibrous osteodystrophy (see Figure 23.7), 
graphic signs of incidental costochondral changes, con-
severe bilateral hip and stifle osteoarthritis, and mild gluteal 
sistent with previous vitamin C deficiency.
tendon calcification (arrowed). This guinea pig was phenotypically 
satin (see Figure 23.6).
Vitamin E and selenium deficiencies
Like vitamin C, vitamin E and selenium are antioxidants, 
and deficiencies have been implicated in musculoskeletal 
pathology. Deficiency in vitamin E can be associated with 
haemorrhage into periarticular skeletal muscle, and com-
bined vitamin E and selenium deficiency can cause a 
necrotizing degenerative myopathy in young animals (Hill 
et al., 2001). Supplementation may or may not be success-
ful, depending on the degree of degeneration and fibrosis. 
Clinically, it is rare to diagnose deficiencies of vitamin E 
and selenium, although they might be suspected on his-
topathological examination.
Multiple deficiencies
In an experimental study, vitamin C deficiency combined 
Guinea pig with osteoarthritic changes of the right 
23.3 with vitamin E and/or selenium deficiencies caused severe 
temporomandibular joint. Although there is some rotation 
myopathy, leading to death in extreme cases. Clinical signs 
of the skull in this view, the differences between the right (patho-
logical) and left (normal) temporomandibular joints (arrowed) can  of weakness, joint haemorrhage, paralysis and death 
be seen. occurred within 2 months (Hill et al., 2003).

If there is minimal change after 7 days, or if the swelling  for the life of the guinea pig. Affected animals have signifi-
is extensive or focal, radiographs are indicated. Osteomy- cantly shorter lives than unaffected conspecifics.
elitis is not uncommon and is highly unlikely to respond to 
medical management, so prompt diagnosis is important 
(see Figure 23.5). If osteomyelitis is present, amputation 
of the affected digit is recommended. Dehiscence is a 
common complication of digital amputation, so the owners 
should be prepared in case the wound must heal by sec-
ondary intention. If multiple digits have evidence of osteo-
myelitis, euthanasia should be considered.
The prognosis can range from good/excellent (grades 
I–III) to grave (grade V). Owners should be made aware 
that bandage changes will be frequently required and that 
resolution of pododermatitis can take several weeks in 
more complex cases. It is common for more than one foot 
to be involved initially (sharing the same predisposing 
causes) and for worsening pododermatitis to develop in 
contralateral feet due to differential weight-bearing. Suc-
cessful case outcomes rely on properly addressing the 
predisposing factors.
Osteodystrophy
Osteodystrophy was initially reported only in satin guinea 
pigs (Figure 23.6) but has since been seen in animals lack- 23.6 Satin guinea pig. Note the characteristic highly lustrous, 
glossy appearance of the fur.
ing the satin trait. Animals with osteodystrophy show a

the daily provision of a source of ultraviolet light to augment 
vitamin D metabolism. Fatty acid supplementation can be 
considered, as it is used in other species for the treatment 
of glomerulonephropathy.
When a guinea pig is diagnosed with osteodystrophy, 
the owners should be counselled that the animal will have 
a shorter than normal lifespan, with dental disease and 
arthritic changes leading to the need for palliative manage-
ment from early on. The cost of ongoing dental procedures,

Spinal pathology
Intervertebral osteoarthritis and spondylosis are common 
findings in middle-aged to older guinea pigs. This pathol-
ogy can be seen incidentally while taking radiographs for 
other reasons or may be the focus of diagnostic investiga-
tion in a guinea pig showing clinical signs of discomfort, 
urinary and faecal soiling, and poor ambulation.
Spinal sequelae of trauma can include bruising of the 
spinal cord or spinal muscles, or vertebral malalignment 
due to fracture or dislocation. If there is significant paresis
